[{"motor_ref":"f726d713","motor_type":"DragDropCategories","motor_json":"{\"problemText\":\"A piezoelectric buzzer is a small device that makes a continuous piercing sound when connected to a battery. In this demonstration, a piezoelectric buzzer is mounted inside a foam ball on the end of a string. The experimenter swings the ball and buzzer around their head, counter-clockwise when viewed from above.\\n\\n\\n[center][img]http:\\/\\/cdn.artofproblemsolving.com\\/school\\/crypt\\/00540-e12599cedadfdc40445dca22ddd227e6417975d2\\/files\\/13_hw_buzzer_string_gif.gif[\\/img]\\n[size=10]By UNSW PhysClips, https:\\/\\/www.animations.physics.unsw.edu.au\\/labs\\/doppler\\/doppler_lab.html[\\/size][\\/center]\\n\\nA nearby microphone records the sound and produces this graph of frequency versus time:\\n\\n\\n[center][img]http:\\/\\/cdn.artofproblemsolving.com\\/school\\/crypt\\/00540-e12599cedadfdc40445dca22ddd227e6417975d2\\/files\\/13_hw_buzzer_string_data.png[\\/img]\\n[size=10]By UNSW PhysClips, https:\\/\\/www.animations.physics.unsw.edu.au\\/labs\\/doppler\\/doppler_lab.html[\\/size][\\/center]\\n\\n\\n[b]Drag and drop each frequency value to match the motion of the buzzer that produces the value.[\\/b]\\n\",\"barChartText\":null,\"type\":\"DragDropCategories\",\"parseFormat\":\"draggablesInEachDropArea\",\"outputDisplayFormat\":\"formattedList\",\"barChartFormat\":null,\"options\":{\"categoryNames\":[\"Toward microphone\",\"Sideways compared to microphone\",\"Away from microphone\"],\"draggableContents\":[\"$3{,}150\\\\;\\\\mathrm{Hz}$\",\"$3{,}290\\\\;\\\\mathrm{Hz}$\",\"$3{,}450\\\\;\\\\mathrm{Hz}$\"],\"areDraggablesReusable\":false,\"height\":200},\"problem_text_fmt\":\"A piezoelectric buzzer is a small device that makes a continuous piercing sound when connected to a battery. In this demonstration, a piezoelectric buzzer is mounted inside a foam ball on the end of a string. The experimenter swings the ball and buzzer around their head, counter-clockwise when viewed from above.
\\n

\\nBy UNSW PhysClips, https:\\/\\/www.animations.physics.unsw.edu.au\\/labs\\/doppler\\/doppler_lab.html<\\/span>\\n<\\/div>\\n
\\nA nearby microphone records the sound and produces this graph of frequency versus time:
\\n
\\n\\n
\\n
\\nBy UNSW PhysClips, https:\\/\\/www.animations.physics.unsw.edu.au\\/labs\\/doppler\\/doppler_lab.html<\\/span>\\n<\\/div>\\n
\\n
\\nDrag and drop each frequency value to match the motion of the buzzer that produces the value.<\\/b>
\\n\"}"}]
solution_text:
When the buzzer is moving away from the microphone, the wavelength is increased and the frequency is decreased. Motion away from the microphone will coincide with the lowest frequency value of $3{,}150\;\mathrm{Hz}\,.$ 

When the buzzer is moving sideways with respect to the microphone, the Doppler effect is the least. The frequency is the in-between value of $3{,}290\;\mathrm{Hz}\,.$

When the buzzer is moving toward the microphone, the wavelength is decreased and the frequency is increased. This coincides with the highest value of $3{,}450\;\mathrm{Hz}\,.$
